title: Hirschsprung Disease
chief_complaint: Newborn hasn't passed meconium in 48 hours. Now he's vomiting green fluid, his belly is distended, and he's not tolerating feeds.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Severely distended, tympanitic. Visible bowel loops. No tenderness currently.
physical_exam.Rectal: Empty rectal vault on digital exam. Explosive release of gas and liquid stool when finger withdrawn (blast sign - classic for Hirschsprung).

hidden_data.abdominal_xr: Distended bowel loops with air-fluid levels. No free air. Transition zone at rectosigmoid (narrow distal segment, dilated proximal colon).
hidden_data.contrast_enema: Transition zone at rectosigmoid (narrow distal segment). Dilated proximal colon. Rectal diameter < sigmoid diameter. Delayed evacuation of contrast on 24h film (retained barium) - PATHOGNOMONIC.
hidden_data.rectal_biopsy: Suction rectal biopsy: Absence of ganglion cells in submucosal and myenteric plexuses. Hypertrophied nerve trunks. Acetylcholinesterase staining: Increased staining of nerve fibers (confirmatory).
hidden_data.genetic: RET gene mutation found. Associated with Down syndrome in 10% (not present here).

correct_diagnosis: Hirschsprung Disease (Congenital Aganglionic Megacolon) - Rectosigmoid (Short Segment)
key_learning_points: Hirschsprung = congenital absence of ganglion cells in distal bowel (Auerbach and Meissner plexuses) → failure of relaxation → functional obstruction. 80% present in neonatal period

Pathophysiology: Failure of neural crest cell migration during embryogenesis. Aganglionic segment always starts at anus and extends proximally. Short segment (rectosigmoid) in 80%. Long segment in 15%. Total colonic aganglionosis in 5%

Classic triad: Delayed meconium passage (>48h in term infant) + Abdominal distension + Bilious vomiting. Blast sign on rectal exam (explosive stool). Enterocolitis is feared complication (can be fatal) - presents with fever, explosive diarrhea, sepsis

Diagnosis: Contrast enema (transition zone, retained barium at 24h). Suction rectal biopsy (gold standard) - absence of ganglion cells + hypertrophied nerves + increased AChE staining. Anorectal manometry (absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex)

Treatment: Initial: NG decompression, rectal irrigations (10mL/kg NSS q4-6h) to decompress. Definitive: Surgical resection of aganglionic segment + pull-through procedure (Soave, Duhamel, Swenson). Timing: Elective at 3-6 months. Emergency if enterocolitis or perforation. Prognosis: 95% achieve fecal continence
